explanation: Technically any amphibian
but most likely used for frogs and toads.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.
The one honest DSP handle is repetition: frog and toad calls are pulse trains, so onset_periodicity and onset_rate run higher than for a one-shot mammal call. That narrows nothing about the species.
A dense multi-animal frog chorus belongs to AMBIENCE, not here; this subcategory expects foregrounded individual calls.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

explanation: All manner of marine and sea animals
not just mammals
but anything that lives in water.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

This subcategory is acoustically incoherent by construction ('anything that lives in water'): it spans whale song (long tonal sweeps), dolphin click trains (impulsive, ultrasonic-adjacent), seal barks (voiced) and crustacean snaps (impulsive). No single Gaussian describes it, so the priors here are deliberately wide and weak and the tier is semantic_only. Text decides.
Underwater/hydrophone material is often band-limited; band_limit_high_hz is a weak nudge, nothing more.

explanation: Bat chirps
echolocation and communicative vocalization. Wings would go under WINGS-MISC.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

Bat material is a fast train of very short, very high-frequency clicks/chirps, so the priors skew hard to high spectral_centroid, high high_band_energy and high onset_rate. That is a real morphological tendency, but it does not distinguish a bat from a rodent squeak, an insect stridulation or a high bird chirp - all of which land in the same corner of the feature space. Hence semantic_only.
Native echolocation is ultrasonic and only exists in a library after heterodyning or time-expansion, which destroys the original spectral statistics; do not trust frequency priors on such files.
Wing flaps belong to WINGS-MISC per the explanation, not here.

explanation: Domesticated house cats. See also ANIMALS-CAT WILD.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

CAT DOMESTIC vs CAT WILD is the clearest example in UCS of a boundary DSP cannot draw. 'Domesticated' is not an acoustic property. A house-cat yowl and a leopard call are the same physics with different vocal-tract lengths, and the ranges overlap so heavily that any numeric boundary would be a fabrication. The body-size pitch prior below is a WEAK nudge (small cat -> somewhat higher f0) and must never be read as species identification.
Purr is an exception with real structure - a low, ~25 Hz amplitude-modulated rumble - but WILD cats purr too, so it still does not settle the boundary.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

explanation: Wild cats as opposed to domesticated cats. Tigers and Lions.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

'Wild cats as opposed to domesticated cats' is a semantic distinction, not an acoustic one. The only defensible signal correlate is body size: a lion or tiger has a far longer vocal tract than a house cat, so f0 and formants sit lower and low_band_energy is higher. That is a WEAK, wide prior - a big-cat purr or hiss overlaps a house cat completely. Do not assert this subcategory from signal.
A big-cat roar is loud, low, rough and long; if pitch is above ~600 Hz the file is more likely ANMLCat, but this is a tendency, not a gate.

explanation: All Domesticated dogs
excluding wild dogs. Pet dogs. See also ANIMALS-WILD DOG for non-domesticated dogs.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

DOG vs DOG WILD (wolves, foxes, coyotes) is pure taxonomy. A bark and a wolf howl are both animal vocalizations; nothing in a spectral feature set says 'domesticated'. A fabricated numeric boundary here would be worse than none, because someone would trust it. semantic_only, and the synonyms carry the decision.

The only weak, honest tendency: pet-dog material is bark-dominated (short, percussive, high crest_factor) while wild-canid material is howl-dominated (long, sustained, tonal, low crest_factor). Both animals do both. Treat as a nudge only.

NOTE: this subcategory's synonyms list in UCS 8.2.1 is corrupt ('UCS marke'), so text matching cannot fire for it. Until that is fixed, DOG is effectively unreachable by the classifier - flag it rather than compensating with invented priors.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

explanation: Wild dogs and canines like Wolves
Foxes
Coyotes etc.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.
Wolves, foxes, coyotes, jackals, hyenas. Acoustically these ARE domestic dogs - the same larynx, the same call repertoire. No DSP feature separates them. semantic_only, decided by synonyms.

Weak tendency only: howl-type material is longer, more sustained and more harmonic than a bark, so length_seconds and sustain_ratio lean higher than ANMLDog. A fox scream or a coyote yip is short and destroys even that tendency.

explanation: Domesticated mammals you'd find on a farm. For farm birds see BIRDS-FOWL. See also AMBIENCE-FARM.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

'Domesticated mammals you'd find on a farm' is a semantic set, not an acoustic one: it lumps a cow (very low, sustained), a pig (mid, rough, squealing), a goat and a sheep (mid, bleating, strongly modulated). No unified signature exists. semantic_only.

Legitimate weak prior: cattle are large, so f0 and centroid sit low and low_band_energy is high. That correlate is real physics (vocal-tract length) but it is shared with HORSE and with big wild mammals, so it is body size, never species.
Farm BIRDS go to BIRDS-FOWL; dense yard scenes go to AMBIENCE-FARM. If the file is a stationary multi-source bed rather than a foregrounded call, it is an ambience.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

explanation: All members of the horse family
including donkeys and mules. See also FOOTSTEPS-HORSE and EQUIPMENT-BRIDLE.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

'All members of the horse family, including donkeys and mules' - explicitly a taxonomic set. A whinny, a bray and a nicker share nothing that DSP could bind together and separate from cattle. semantic_only.

Body-size prior only: a horse is large, so f0 skews low. A whinny is a strongly frequency-modulated harmonic call (high spectral_flux with high harmonicity), which is a mild tendency, not an identifier.

Hoofbeats are FOOTSTEPS-HORSE and tack is EQUIPMENT-BRIDLE, per the explanation. A file that is a rhythmic impact train with no vocalization should be scored there, not here - and THAT split IS DSP-decidable.

explanation: All manner of traditional insect sounds
crickets
cicada
flies and bees. And singular insect recordings. See also AMBIENCE-INSECT now for dense ambience style recordings.

provenance.reason: the reasoned gate rejected 5% of this subcategory's own true clips

discriminators: Unlike the rest of ANIMALS, this one has a genuine acoustic identity rather than a taxonomic one: stridulation and wingbeat are mechanical oscillators, so insect material is a high-frequency, near-stationary, strongly periodic texture (cricket chirp trains, cicada buzz) with almost no energy below 200 Hz. That is measurable and prunes hard against every mammal vocalization - hence signal_narrowable, not semantic_only.
It does NOT identify the insect: cricket vs cicada vs katydid vs mosquito is text.

A bee or mosquito flight tone is a sustained_tonal buzz whose pitch IS the wingbeat rate; that material overlaps WINGS-INSECT, and the split is editorial (is the subject the animal, or its wings?), not acoustic.
Dense continuous night-field beds belong to AMBIENCE-INSECT per the explanation; use stationarity and length to prefer AMBIENCE when the file is a minutes-long unchanging bed.

measured_evidence.selection: clips whose FSD50K labels map unambiguously to this one subcategory (high/medium crosswalk confidence only)

explanation: Other animals not finding a clear category.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

The catch-all. It carries the ANIMALS class gates (it is a vocalization, not a drone or an impact) and nothing more. It should win only by abstention, when the file is clearly an animal but no sibling's text evidence fires.
Deliberately given weak, wide priors so it never out-scores a sibling that has real text support.

explanation: All members of the primate family except humans. Apes
Chimps
etc.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.
'All members of the primate family except humans' - taxonomy. A chimp pant-hoot, a gorilla grunt, a gibbon whoop and a lemur call have no shared signature that excludes other mammals. semantic_only.

The one interesting (and still weak) cue: primates are the closest non-human match to the HUMAN vocal apparatus, so voicing_ratio and syllabic_modulation_energy run high - primate material is the most speech-like of all animal material. That is exactly why it is confusable with HUMAN and with CREATURES-HUMANOID (which is often built from primate or human source), and why it cannot be asserted from signal.

explanation: All reptiles including snakes
turtles and lizzards.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

Reptiles are the one ANIMALS subcategory whose typical morphology is NOT voiced: a snake hiss is sustained broadband noise (high spectral_flatness, near-zero harmonicity) and a rattle is a dry high-frequency buzz. That pushes the priors toward the noise corner and is a real tendency - but a crocodile bellow is a low voiced roar, so the class is bimodal and no single set of priors covers it. semantic_only, honestly.
A hiss here is indistinguishable from a cat hiss (ANMLCat/ANMLWcat), from AIR-HISS and from a steam leak. Only text can settle it.

Snakes, turtles and lizards are largely SILENT animals; much 'reptile' library material is actually body movement over substrate, which is closer to VEGETATION/DIRT_SAND friction than to a vocalization.

explanation: All manner 'gnawing' animals like rats and mice. Rodents.

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

Taxonomy again ('gnawing animals'). What IS defensible is the body-size correlate at its strongest: rodents are the smallest mammals in this category, so squeaks sit at the top of the pitch range (often 1-4 kHz, and much rat/mouse vocalization is genuinely ultrasonic and only present in a library after pitch-shifting). Short, high, bright events. That is a real WEAK prior, and it is the opposite pole from HORSE/FARM.
It still does not identify a rodent: a bat chirp, a small bird call and a fledgling squeak occupy the same region. semantic_only.
Gnawing/scratching material is a friction/impact texture, not a vocalization, and will fail the voiced priors - expect it to abstain toward ANMLMisc or to score against WOOD/PAPER friction instead.

explanation: Any wild animal not explicitly defined by the list
and not domesticated. Wolves and Coyotes now to go ANIMALS-DOG WILD

discriminators: Signal analysis can establish that this is an ANIMAL VOCALIZATION (voiced/harmonic-with-noise, non-stationary, 0.1-3 s events, formant-ish mid-band structure) and can therefore rule out drones, impacts, machines and stationary ambiences. It cannot establish the SPECIES. No spectral-centroid/MFCC feature says 'domesticated'. Species identity here is carried by synonyms text, not by DSP.

Explicitly a residual set ('any wild animal not explicitly defined by the list'), spanning a bear, an elephant, a deer and a raccoon. It has no signature, only the class gates. semantic_only, and it should generally lose to any sibling with a text hit.
Per the explanation, wolves and coyotes moved OUT of here to ANIMALS-DOG WILD; do not let this subcategory absorb canid material on signal grounds - it cannot tell the difference anyway.
